segments.s397-g97: This is a compact measurement phrase: 'سه فرسنگ بالا' = 'three parasangs high' (numeral + measure + adjective), followed by 'پهنا چهل' = 'its breadth (was) forty'. Classical Persian often places the numeral after the measure noun (here 'پهنا' before 'چهل') for a terse, poetic effect.
segments.s397-g98: The clause 'بجایی ندید اندر او آب و گل' is negative past: 'ندید' (he did not see) takes the adverbial 'بجایی' ('in any place/anywhere') before it; the prepositional phrase 'اندر او' ('inside it') locates the objects 'آب و گل' (water and mud) that he failed to see. Word order puts the negated verb before the location phrase, a common flexible order in verse.
segments.s397-g99: 'به پهنای دیوار او' is a measure/locative phrase: 'به' + 'پهنای دیوار او' = 'across/along the width of its wall'. 'بر سوار' indicates what was on it — 'riders upon (the wall)'; the preposition 'بر' (= on/upon) governs 'سوار' (horseman/rider).
segments.s397-g100: 'برفتی' is a past narrative verb meaning '(there) rode/they went upon' (classical poetic past), followed by adverbial phrases 'برابر بروبر' (= 'abreast / side by side') and the numeral 'چهار' at the end to specify number. In this idiomatic order the verb precedes the adverbial qualifiers and the numeral is placed for emphasis at the close.
segments.s397-g101: 'چو' here is the conjunction 'when'; the clause follows with the proper name subject 'اسفندیار', the object 'آن شگفتی' ('that wonder/marvel') and the past verb 'بدید' ('saw'). Word order (conjunction + subject + object + verb) is typical of classical narrative lines.
segments.s397-g102: This is an idiomatic expressive clause: 'یکی' = 'a/one' (indefinite), 'باد سرد' = 'a cold wind' (noun + adjective), 'از جگر' = 'from the heart/liver' (a poetic locution meaning from deep within), and 'برکشید' is the past transitive verb 'he drew up/raised' (here meaning he let out/uttered). The verb takes 'باد' as its object.
segments.s397-g103: 'چنین گفت' = 'he said thus' (past reporting). 'کاین را' ('that this' + object marker 'را') introduces the clause; 'نشاید' is an impersonal/modal present meaning 'it is not fitting/it must not', and 'ستد' is the verb 'to seize/take' (infinitive/indicative in construction with نشاید), so the phrase means 'that should not be taken/seized.'
segments.s397-g104: 'بد آمد' is a verbal phrase 'evil/bad came' = 'misfortune befell'; 'به روی من' literally 'to my face' functions idiomatically as 'upon me'; and 'از راه بد' gives the source or manner 'by/from an evil/bad way' (i.e. by ill fortune or ill means). The sequence is past verb + experiencer phrase + source phrase.
segments.s397-g73: همه (preposed quantifier) = “all” modifying گورشان; گورشان is a possessive construction (گور + ـشان ‘their’) meaning “their graves.” کام here means “satisfaction/fulfillment” and شیران is the noun “lions” (literal beasts) in the genitive-like phrase کام شیران = “the satisfaction (or meal) of the lions.” کنم is the 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/optative form of کردن used as a volitional/future-like “I will make/do,” so the clause says “I will make all their graves (to be) the lions’ satisfaction.”
segments.s397-g74: به کام introduces the beneficiary/goal with به = “for/to” and کام again means “satisfaction/pleasure.” دلیران ایران is a noun phrase “the brave ones of Iran” where دلیران is plural “braves/valiant men” and ایران specifies which group; کنم repeats the first‑person volitional “I will make.” The whole phrase means “I will make (them) for the pleasure/satisfaction of Iran’s brave men.”
segments.s397-g75: سراسر is an adverb/adverbial phrase “throughout/through and through,” modifying the verb بدوزم. بدوزم is the 1st‑person singular present‑subjunctive/optative of دوختن used here as a volitional “I will (do the action)”—literally “I will stitch/sew,” poetically “I will pierce/stab.” جگرشان (جگر + ـشان) is “their hearts/livers” (seat of feeling), and به تیر = “with arrows” uses به in the instrumental sense “by/with.”
segments.s397-g76: بیارم is the 1st‑person present‑subjunctive/optative of آوردن (“to bring”) used volitionally “I will bring / take” (the lemma supplied in the data seems mismatched); زن و کودکانشان is the coordinated direct object “their women and children” (کودکان + ـشان), and اسیر is a predicative adjective meaning “captives.” The clause thus says “I will bring (them) — their women and children — as captives.”
segments.s397-g77: ترا (here segmented as ت + را) is the old-style object form “you” (ت second-person element + را the object marker). شاد خوانیم uses خوانیم (1st‑person plural present/subjunctive of خواندن) in the sense “we call/name” (خواندن can mean ‘to name’), so ترا شاد خوانیم = “we will call you happy.” ازین‌گر is a conjunctive adverbial “from this / if because of this” linking a concessive/conditional thought, and دژم is an adjectival predicate “sullen/grim.”
segments.s397-g78: بگوی is an imperative/optative form of گفتن “say” (the provided lemma senses are nouny and do not match the verbal use here), commanding the addressee to speak. آنچ = “that which” (relative/indefinite pronoun), داری is 2nd‑person present “you have,” and به دل بیش و کم = “in your heart, more or less” (به + دل indicates where/for which, بیش و کم is a fixed expression meaning “more or less”). The clause asks the addressee to tell whatever he has in his heart, whether more or less.
segments.s397-g79: دل is the nominative/subject “the heart.” گرگسار is a poetic adjective/epithet (literally ‘wolf‑haunted’ or ‘wolfish’); here it conveys ferocity. اندر + ان = اندرَ(ین) / اندرَان “in/among them” (archaic locative), so اندرَان means “within/among them.” تنگ شد is a two-word predicate: تنگ “narrow/tight” + شد “became,” so “became tight/contracted.” The clause describes the heart becoming constricted/fierce internally.
segments.s397-g80: روان here means “spirit/soul,” and زبانش is “his tongue/speech” (زبان + ـش ‘his’). پر is used as an adjective “full (of),” آژنگ (a poetic noun) means “clang/clamor” (a loud harsh sound), and شد is the auxiliary “became.” The coordination روان و زبانش ... شد indicates both his spirit and his speech became filled with noisy clamor or tumultuous utterance.
segments.s397-g17: نگفتی is the past 2nd‑person form with the negative prefix ن- and here functions rhetorically as 'Didn't you say...'. که introduces the reported clause. ایدر نیابی تو آب is inverted poetic word order: ایدر 'here' + نیابی (present/subjunctive/potential 'you will not find' or 'you would not find') + تو (emphatic second‑person pronoun) + آب 'water' — literally 'here find‑not you water', i.e. 'you would find no water here.'
segments.s397-g18: بسوزد is a present/jussive form (the b‑ prefix plus سوخت) expressing potential/result 'will/may burn' (3rd person). ترا is the clitic object (ت + را) meaning 'you' as object. تابش آفتاب is the subject phrase 'the sun's radiance' placed after the verb (poetic order): literally 'may the sun's shining burn you.'
segments.s397-g19: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کردی‌ای is the past 2nd‑person کرد(ی) 'you did' with the archaic/vocative particle ­ای attached for address/exclamation (so 'you did, O ...'). بدتن is an epithet 'ill‑bodied' / 'wretched in body' addressing the person. از آب خاک lists elements ('from/of water, (of) earth/dust') in parataxis; the clause is elliptical (object is supplied by the next segment).
segments.s397-g20: سپه را is the definite object marked with را ('the army'). همه emphasizes totality ('the whole/entire'). کرده بودی is the compound past (کرده + بودی) giving a pluperfect/result: 'you had made/caused' (i.e. 'you had brought about'). هلاک is the predicate/noun 'ruin/annihilation' describing the army's fate ('had made the whole army destroyed').
segments.s397-g21: چنین داد پاسخ is an idiomatic sequence 'gave such an answer' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). که introduces the reported clause. مرگ سپاه is a noun‑phrase 'the death of the army/host' functioning as the subject of the reported clause.
segments.s397-g22: مرا روشناییست is older Persian word order: مرا (to/for me — here split in the manuscript into م + را) is the dative/experiential object 'for me'; روشناییست is روشنایی + است contracted to mean 'is a brightness/gleam'. چون introduces a simile 'like', and هور و ماه are the two terms of comparison ('splendor/sun‑glory' and 'the moon').
segments.s397-g23: چه introduces a rhetorical question 'what'. بینم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'do I see' used in questions. همی is an archaic/emphatic particle meaning 'still' or continuous aspect. از تو literally 'from you' functions here as 'in you' or 'of you'. جز introduces the exception 'except', and پای‌بند is a compound adjective 'bound; loyal; restrained' used as the only quality seen in the addressee.
segments.s397-g24: چه خواهم ترا is another rhetorical question ('what should I want of you?') with چه + خواهم (present/future 'I will/would want') and ترا (ت + را) as the clitic object 'you'. جز introduces the lone exception 'except', followed by two nouns linked by و: بلا 'calamity/affliction' and گزند 'harm/injury' — i.e. 'I could desire nothing from you but calamity and harm.'
segments.s397-g49: سپهبد (the subject) + بفرمود (honorific past verb) = 'the spahbed ordered/said'. تا introduces a subordinate (purpose/that-) clause whose finite verb appears later; مشگ آب (two nouns in close apposition, poetic word order) is the object/thing to be scattered — literally 'musk (into) water', with the target 'water' expressed immediately after 'musk' rather than by a separate prepositional phrase.
segments.s397-g50: بریزند is third-person plural subjunctive/present (used after تا following an order) = 'that they pour/scatter'. The following prepositional phrases در آب و در ماهتاب specify the locations/targets ('in the water and in the moonlight'); verb-first order is a common poetic inversion here.
segments.s397-g51: به دریا is a fronted prepositional phrase 'towards/into the sea'. سبک‌بار شد is an adjectival predicate with شد (past of شدن) meaning 'became/was made light' = 'was lightened'. بارگی (here 'baggage/load') is the thing that became light; poetic word order places the destination before the predicate.
segments.s397-g52: سپاه (the host) is subject and آمد the past verb 'came/entered'. اندر indicates movement 'into/within' (here into the sea is implied). به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'all at once/suddenly' (به used as an adverbial marker + یکبارگی).
segments.s397-g53: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when (they) came'. به خشکی = 'to the dry land/shore' (خشکی used for the land opposite the sea). سپاه و بنه is a compound noun phrase 'the army and its baggage/encampment' (و linking the two), with the object/participants stated before the predicate as typical in verse.
segments.s397-g54: ببد is an archaic/past form equivalent to بود/شده = 'was/became'. میسره (predicate) means 'attainable/passable' or 'easy to traverse'. راست با میمنه specifies the side: راست 'to the right' and با میمنه 'with the right side' — together indicating that the right-hand route became passable; word order is compressed and idiomatic.
segments.s397-g55: به نزدیک (toward the vicinity of) + رویین دژ (an adjective + noun 'iron fortress') forms a prepositional phrase placed before the verb for emphasis. آمد سپاه is the verb 'came' followed by its subject 'the army' (inverted order typical of poetry): 'the army came near the iron fortress.'
segments.s397-g56: چنان شد که introduces a result clause 'so it came about that...'. فرسنگ ده is a numeric phrase 'ten farsangs' with noun + numeral in poetic order, and ماند راه ('remained [of] road') uses ماند 'was left/remaining' with راه 'road' as the thing remaining — 'ten farsangs of road remained'.
segments.s397-g81: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو is the indirect object 'to him' placed before the verb گفت (past 'said'), a common Persian order. تا چند گویی چنین is an interrogative time/extent phrase: تا چند = 'how long/until when', گویی (here functioning like 'you say' or 'you keep saying') follows, and چنین 'such / like that' modifies the speech.
segments.s397-g82: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause ('that / lest'); بر تو = 'against/upon you'; مبادا is an optative/negative-wish 'may it not be / lest', so the whole clause expresses a curse or wish that something not happen to you. به داد آفرین is a compound-like phrase invoking the 'Just‑Creator' (داد = justice, آفرین ≈ maker/creator), here the addressee of the curse.
segments.s397-g83: A short optative curse: همه اختر بد = 'every/ all ill star' (اختر = star, بد = ill/unfavorable). به جان تو باد uses به + noun + تو and the optative باد ('may') to mean 'may (this) be upon your life' — literally 'may every ill star be to/for your life'.
segments.s397-g84: Parallel optative formula: بریده is a past participle/adjective 'cut off' or 'cut through'; به خنجر (instrumental) = 'by a dagger' (به here used in the instrumental sense); میان = 'middle/through the middle'; تو باد with باد again gives the optative 'may you be ...'. The clause curses that he be cut through the middle by a dagger.
segments.s397-g85: به خاک اندر افگنده is a prepositional + verb cluster: به خاک اندر = 'onto/into the earth' (اندر is a poetic 'in/into'), and افگنده is a past participle/verb 'cast/thrown'. پر خون تنت is a descriptive noun phrase 'your body full of blood' (پر + noun = 'full of blood', تنت = 'your body'), placed after the verb for poetic order.
segments.s397-g86: A verbless nominal clause stating the resulting condition: زمین بستر = 'the earth [was] (your) bed' and گرد پیراهنت = 'and dust [was] your shirt/garment' (گرد = dust; پیراهنت = 'your shirt'). Persian often omits the copula in such poetic statements.
segments.s397-g87: ز گفتار او = 'from his speech' (ز is the poetic 'from'); تیز شد = 'became sharp/keen' where تیز figuratively means quick‑tempered or roused; نامدار follows the verb as the subject ('Namdār' or 'the famed one'), showing common Persian verb–subject inversion used for effect.
segments.s397-g88: برآشفت is a past/single action verb 'became angry / sprang up in rage'. با تنگدل is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'with narrow heart' (treating تنگدل as an epithet = 'narrow‑hearted'), and گرگسار is a proper name or epithet ('Gorgesār' / 'wolf‑like, ferocious'), together meaning 'the narrow‑hearted Gorgesār flew into a rage.'
segments.s397-g25: سپهبد (title) is the subject placed before the verb. بخندید is a past 3rd‑person singular (classical/poetic form) meaning “laughed.” و links two coordinated verbal clauses; بگشاد is the past 3rd singular of گشودن (here ‘opened’) followed by its object چشم (eye). The verb carries the b‑prefix (classical verbal form) and the simple S‑V‑O idea is slightly inverted because two verbs are conjoined.
segments.s397-g26: فرو ماند is a compound of the adverb فرو (“down”) + ماند (past/remain) meaning roughly “fell down / was kept down” (i.e. his eyes dropped). زان = ز + آن = “from that; because of that,” pointing back to the Turk. ترک here names the person (Turk). و + بفزود خشم is a second coordinated clause: بفزود is an additive/causative past form “caused to increase” and خشم is its object “anger.”
segments.s397-g27: بدو is the prepositional/dative form meaning “to him” and precedes the verb (common in classical Persian: object/indirect object before the verb). گفت here functions as the verb ‘said’ (classical past); کای is the vocative particle “O.” کم خرد is a compound adjective (literally “little‑wisdom”) meaning “of little wit / foolish,” with کم qualifying خرد (wisdom). گرگسار is an epithet apposed to the vocative—‘wolfish’ or ‘ferocious’—addressing the addressee.
segments.s397-g28: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause: “when/if.” پیروز is a predicate adjective meaning “victorious,” placed before the verb. گردم is the 1st‑person singular classical verbal form (become) — here “I become/will be.” من is the pronoun ‘I’ (often redundant in Persian but used for emphasis), and از کارزار is a prepositional phrase “from the battlefield.” Word order is predicate + verb + pronoun + complement, typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s397-g29: به رویین دژت is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/for your iron fortress” (رویین = ‘iron‑hard’, دژت = ‘your fortress’ with possessive ‑ت). بر + سپهبد کنم is the verb phrase: کنم is 1st‑person singular (classical present/subjunctive used for future/optative sense) “I will make/do,” and سپهبد is the title ‘spahbed’ as the complement—so the sense is “I will make (you) spahbed over/at your iron fortress.” The personal object (you) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s397-g30: مبادا که is an optative/negative formula meaning “may it not be that / lest.” The following clause uses هرگز (never) + بتو (to you) + بد (bad/harm) + کنم (1st‑person verb) to express the speaker’s wish that he never do harm to the addressee. Note the negated optative in the matrix (مبادا) governs the subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s397-g31: همه پادشاهی سراسر is a noun phrase: همه (the whole/entire) modifies پادشاهی (kingdom), with سراسر reinforcing ‘throughout/entire.’ تراست is an archaic contraction of ترا است = ‘it is yours,’ with the predicate coming after the noun phrase as is common in Persian (noun phrase + copula).
segments.s397-g32: چو introduces a conditional ‘if/when.’ با ما کنی uses کنی (2nd‑person subjunctive) with با ما ‘with us’ meaning ‘you act/deal with us’; the verb implies behavior or treatment. در سخن راه راست is a prepositional phrase ‘in speech, a straight/right way’ (در = in, سخن = speech, راه راست = the right/straight path) specifying the manner; together the clause means ‘if you speak/behave towards us straightforwardly.’
segments.s397-g65: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning “when.” از + noun (از تن) literally “from the body” precedes the verb. ببرم is an archaic/poetic first-person form (optative/subjunctive) of بردن meaning “I take / I sever”; the object سر ارجاسپ را (head + proper name + object marker را) follows the verb, so word order is temporal clause → prepositional phrase → verb → object.
segments.s397-g66: درخشان is an adjective used predicatively (“bright/shining”) and کنَم (کنم) is the first-person present/optative of کردن meaning “I make / cause.” Together they form a causative predicate: “I will make (something) shining.” جان لهراسپ را is the definite object (soul of Lohrasp) marked by را and follows the verb phrase, a typical classical Persian order (adjective + verb + object).
segments.s397-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” کهرم here functions as a nominal (likely a proper name in the poem); که begins a relative clause modifying that noun (“who/that”). از خون فرشیدورد uses از with خون to indicate lineage or origin (“of the blood of Farshidvard”), so the relative clause reads “who is of Farshidvard’s blood.”
segments.s397-g68: دل لشکری is a compound noun phrase: دل modified by لشکری (martial/military) — “the army’s (or warlike) heart.” کرد is the past-tense verb “made/caused,” and پر here is an adjectival complement meaning “full (of).” The nouns خون و درد follow as the things the heart is filled with: “filled with blood and sorrow/pain.”
segments.s397-g69: دگر here means “another” (introducing a further person). اندریمان is the personal name (subject). که introduces a relative clause “who…,” and پیروز گشت is a verbal phrase with پیروز adjectival/predicative (“victorious”) and گشت (past tense of گشتن) meaning “became,” so the clause reads “who became victorious.”
segments.s397-g70: بکشت is an archaic/poetic preterite form meaning “he killed” (the b‑ prefix appears in older/perhaps dialectal forms); it governs a partitive phrase which follows: از دلیران ما سی و هشت = “thirty‑eight of our brave (men).” از marks the partitive/genitive source, دلیران ما is “our brave ones,” and the numeral follows in the classical order.
segments.s397-g71: سرانشان is the plural noun سران (“heads, leaders”) with the possessive suffix ‑شان “their,” serving as the object. ببرم is first‑person subjunctive/optative of بردن (“I will take / I will carry off / I will sever”). به کین نیا places purpose/attitude (به کین = “in/for vengeance”) with نیا (“ancestor”) specifying whose vengeance is invoked: “for the ancestor’s vengeance.”
segments.s397-g72: پدید آرم is an archaic/binomial verbal expression: پدید (from پدید آمدن/پدید آوردن) “to bring forth/manifest” plus آرم, an older 1st‑person form corresponding to ‘I bring’/‘I will bring,’ yielding “I will bring forth.” از هر دری is a prepositional phrase “from every (sea/quarter)” and کیمیا is the object (here “the elixir” or “secret elixir”), so the phrase means “I will produce the elixir from every sea/region.”
segments.s397-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here ('when'). یک پاس is an indefinite measure — ‘one watch’ (a guard‑shift/period) — and بگذشت is past tense ‘passed’ used in the sense of time passing. از تیره شب is a prepositional/time phrase ('from/through the dark night') placed after the verb phrase for poetic word order.
segments.s397-g2: به پیش اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning 'came forward' (به پیش = toward the front, اندر آمد = came in/appeared). خروش جلب is a noun phrase 'the uproar (خروش) of جلب'; in this line جلب functions as a poetic substantive linked to خروش (its exact sense is contextually determined). The verb آمد precedes the noun phrase for emphasis, a common poetic order.
segments.s397-g3: بخندید is third‑person past 'laughed' and with the preposition بر here takes a target: بخندید بر X = 'laughed at X'. بارگی شاه نو is the target phrase ('the foliage/leaf of the new king') placed after بر.
segments.s397-g4: ز دم سپه رفت is a temporal/source phrase plus verb: ز introduces source/time ('from/at the moment of'), دم here means 'moment' and سپه 'the army'; رفت is past 'went'. تا پیش رو gives the limit/direction 'as far as/to the front' (تا marks the extent). Together: 'at the army's moment he went up to the front.'
segments.s397-g5: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. چون here introduces a temporal clause ('as/when'); پیش لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'before the army/host' and کشید (past of کشیدن) means 'drew up/advanced' (the supplied sense list lacks the usual 'to draw' meaning, but here کشید is the verb 'to pull/draw/advance'). The clause describes the commander advancing to the front of the host.
segments.s397-g6: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'a certain/one', modifying ژرف دریای بی‌بن ('a depth of the sea without bottom'), and بدید is simple past 'he saw'. Word order is noun phrase then verb (typical in poetic narration): 'he saw a deep of the bottomless sea.'
segments.s397-g7: هیونی is a noun 'a beast/monstrous creature' and is modified by the relative clause که بود اندران کاروان ('that was among the caravan'). که introduces the relative clause and بود is past 'was'; اندران is the old/poetic locative 'among/in them'.
segments.s397-g8: This is an address/question directed at the caravan‑leader. کجا is the interrogative 'where'; پیش رو is a compound noun 'the one who goes before' / 'vanguard'; داشتی is second‑person past 'you had/possessed'; ساروان at the end is a vocative 'O caravan‑leader'. The order places the vocative last for force.
segments.s397-g113: بپرسید is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural narrative verb (they asked) using the classical preverb بـ; و simply links the two verbs as 'and'. گفت is the past verb 'said' continuing the narration (the listed senses did not include 'said', so that specific sense must be inferred). این دژ نامدار is a noun phrase 'this renowned fortress' with the demonstrative این before the noun and the adjective نامدار postpositive.
segments.s397-g114: چه جایست is an interrogative clause: چه 'what' + جایست (جای + است) 'is (a) place' — an archaic copular construction meaning 'what sort of place is this'. و links the second question. چندست is another interrogative 'how many are there' (چند + است). بر وی سوار is a prepositional phrase 'upon it (بر وی)' with سوار 'riders/horsemen' as the (understood) noun counted by چندست.
segments.s397-g115: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/with' introducing the other party (ز ارجاسپ = 'with Arjasp' or 'of Arjasp'); چندی is an adverb 'for a while'. سخن راندند is a poetic collocation meaning 'they uttered/spoke words': سخن 'speech/words' + راندند (past, 3rd plural of راندن). In context it means they exchanged words or spoke to Arjasp for a short time.
segments.s397-g116: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; دفتر دژ is a genitive phrase 'the record/book of the fortress' (دفتر 'book/record' + دژ 'fortress'). برو is a small particle here functioning adverbially ('thereupon' / 'then') rather than its literal 'go'; خواندند is past 3rd-person plural 'they read/recited'. Together: 'they all read from the fortress's book.'
segments.s397-g117: که introduces reported content ('that'). بالا و پهنای دژ را combines two nouns 'height (بالا) and breadth (پهنای) of the fortress' with را marking the whole phrase as definite object. ببین is a present/immediate form 'see' used in the reported clause ('that they may see' / 'to see'); in classical style this short form serves the narrative purpose.
segments.s397-g118: This is a pair of directional phrases: دری سوی ایران — 'on one side/toward Iran' — and دگر سوی چین — 'on the other side/toward China.' دگر is 'other/another' and سوی marks direction. The exact sense of دری is archaic/obscure here (it functions to set up 'one side'); the construction as a whole contrasts the two orientations of the fortress.
segments.s397-g119: بدو here is 'to it/into it' (classical packing of به + او) indicating direction 'in/into it'; اندرون is 'inside/within'. تیغ‌زن is a compound noun 'sword-strikers' = 'swordsmen'. سی‌هزار is the numeral 'thirty thousand' modifying the group.
segments.s397-g120: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' (an apposition or clarification of تیغ‌زن). گردنکش is an adjective 'rebellious/headstrong' and و links to the next adjective نامدار 'renowned', both postpositive as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s397-g41: گذرگاه (noun) is the head of the noun phrase 'the passage'; 'این آب دریا' is a determiner + noun cluster modifying گذرگاه (literally 'this water — the sea' = 'this sea-water'). کجاست is the interrogative copula (کجا + است) meaning 'where is'. Word order is head noun followed by its modifiers and ends with the copula-question.
segments.s397-g42: بباید is the classical/peristaltic form of باید ('it is necessary / you must'); here it marks obligation. نمودن is the bare infinitive 'to show' and pairs with بباید to form 'must show'. به ما is the indirect object ('to-us/for-us') and راه راست is the direct object ('the straight/direct road/way'). Note the verb cluster (modal + infinitive) comes before its objects in this poetic ordering.
segments.s397-g43: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him' placed before the verb (classical poetic order). گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic usage). با آهن از آبگیر is an instrumental / source phrase 'with iron / from the ford (water-collection)', specifying manner or origin of approach.
segments.s397-g44: نیابد is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن + یابد) used here to mean 'does not / cannot find' or 'there is no passage'. گذر is the object 'passage'. پر و پیکان تیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'feather and the arrow' (i.e. projectiles); in context it means 'neither feather nor arrow will find a way'. Note the verb (نیابد) precedes its object(s), a common poetic ordering.
segments.s397-g45: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmātan) as subject. فروماند is a classical passive/stative past meaning 'was left / remained' (from فرو ماندن), so together 'Tahmatan remained/was left'. اندر شگفت is a locative/figurative phrase 'in astonishment' (اندر = 'in', شگفت = 'wonder/astonishment').
segments.s397-g46: هم‌اندر here functions adverbially 'at the same time / likewise'. زمان is 'time/moment' specifying when. بند is a physical 'band/strap/fetter' (here a strap or harness element) and او برگرفت is a past-tense verbal clause 'he took up' (برگرفت = took up/raised). The whole segment means 'at that same moment he seized/took up his band/strap.'
segments.s397-g47: به ... اندرون is a locative prepositional phrase 'into / toward the inner part of'. دریای آب is a genitive combination 'sea's water' (i.e. the sea/waters). گرگسار is an adjective or place-name meaning 'wolf-infested / wolf-haunted', modifying the location: 'into the inner sea-waters of the wolf-haunted place.'
segments.s397-g48: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. هیونی is a noun 'beast / monster' (subject). گرفته is the perfective participle 'having seized / having taken' and مهار is the object of that participle 'the rein/bridle' — together 'a beast, having seized the rein, came.' Note the participial modifier follows the noun it qualifies, a typical classical word order.
segments.s397-g89: The clause begins with the indefinite subject 'یکی' ('someone'), followed by the noun phrase 'تیغ هندی' ('Indian sword') where the adjective-like word 'هندی' follows the noun. The verb 'بزد' is a past‑tense 3rd‑person singular (archaic/poetic form) meaning 'struck', and the prepositional phrase 'بر سرش' ('on his head') follows the verb; 'ش' is the enclitic third‑person possessive pronoun.
segments.s397-g90: 'ز تارک' is a prepositional phrase with 'ز' ('from') introducing the noun 'تارک' (here contextually the crown/nape of the head — this sense is not among the provided options). 'به دو نیم شد' is a resultative construction: 'به' + numeral phrase 'دو نیم' ('into two halves') + past verb 'شد' ('became/was made'), meaning 'was split into two halves'. The final 'تا برش' uses 'تا' as a result/so‑that marker and 'برش' as the noun 'cut' (the outcome of the strike).
segments.s397-g91: The verb phrase 'فگندش' is past plural with the enclitic object 'ش' ('them/it/him') and means 'they threw him'; it takes the directional prepositional phrase 'به دریا' ('into the sea'). The adverbial 'هم‌اندر زمان' ('at the same time/at that moment') follows, where 'هم‌اندر' is a poetic compound meaning 'together/at the same' and 'زمان' functions as 'moment'.
segments.s397-g92: This clause has the phrase 'خور ماهیان' functioning as a genitive construction meaning 'the fish's food' (i.e. 'food for the fishes'); here 'خور' is used in the sense related to eating/food rather than 'sun' and is followed by the plural 'ماهیان'. The copulative/predicative verb 'شد' ('became') assigns that role to 'تن بدگمان' where 'تن' ('body') is the theme and 'بدگمان' is an epithet describing the unfortunate/wretched body.
segments.s397-g93: The prepositional phrase 'وزان جایگه' ('from that place') opens the clause; 'وزان' = 'from that'. The noun 'باره' is marked by the object particle 'را' and followed by the verb 'بر نشست' (compound of preposition 'بر' + past verb 'نشست' = 'he set/sat (it) on/placed it'), so syntactically this reads 'he set/placed the bāre on that place.' The verb 'نشست' is past 3rd‑person singular 'sat/settled/placed'.
segments.s397-g94: 'به تندی' is an adverbial phrase ('with haste/quickly') formed with the preposition 'به' + noun 'تندی'. The phrase 'میان یلی را ببست' has 'میان' ('amid/in the middle of') followed by the direct object 'یلی را' ('the hero/valiant one' marked by 'را') and the past verb 'ببست' (here meaning 'he bound/tied' in the 3rd‑person singular). The overall sense: 'he quickly bound the hero in the middle/among them.'
segments.s397-g95: 'به بالا برآمد' is a directional intransitive clause: 'به' + 'بالا' = 'upward', with 'برآمد' (past 3rd‑person) meaning 'he rose/ascended'. The next short clause 'به دژ بنگرید' uses 'به' as a directional/target marker ('toward the fortress'); 'بنگرید' is an archaic/poetic past form of 'نگریستن' meaning 'he looked' or 'he gazed'. The two clauses are coordinated actions.
segments.s397-g96: This is a nominal phrase 'یکی ساده دژ آهنین' meaning 'a certain/simple iron fortress' where 'یکی' is an indefinite determiner ('a certain' or 'one'), 'ساده' and 'آهنین' modify 'دژ' ('fortress'). The adverbial/temporal particle 'باره' (here roughly 'on that occasion' or 'thereupon') precedes the verb 'دید' (past 3rd‑person 'saw'), completing 'there he saw a simple iron fortress.'
segments.s397-g121: همه پیش ارجاسپ چون بنده‌اند is a nominative clause: همه (everyone) is the subject, پیش ارجاسپ = 'in the presence of Arjasp' (prepositional phrase following the subject), and چون بنده‌اند is a simile predicated of that subject ('they are like servants'). بنده‌اند is a 3rd-person plural copula + predicate adjective (an older/poetic form with -اند).
segments.s397-g122: به فرمان و رایش سرافگنده‌اند is a consequent clause describing the same subject: به فرمان و رایش = 'by/at his command and counsel' (به here marks agency or circumstance), and سرافگنده‌اند is the 3rd-person plural predicate meaning 'they are abashed/put to shame.' Note the coordinate object فرمان و رایش modifying the circumstantial phrase.
segments.s397-g123: خورش هست چندانک اندازه نیست: خورش 'food/stew' is the topic with هست 'there is/it exists'; چندانک introduces a degree ('to such an extent'); اندازه نیست literally 'there is no measure/limit.' The clause expresses an exaggerated quantity (poetic hyperbole) with the negated existential 'نیست' following the noun phrase.
segments.s397-g124: به خوشه درون بار اگر تازه نیست is a subordinate/qualifying clause. به خوشه درون بار strings spatial/genitive elements together: به + خوشه ('to/in the ear') + درون ('inside/within') + بار ('load/sheaf'); اگر تازه نیست is a conditional add-on ('if it is not fresh'). In poetry the order is compact and some elements are elliptical (the exact relation of this clause to the previous line is concessive/conditional).
segments.s397-g125: اگر در ببندد به ده سال شاه is a protasis (conditional clause). اگر 'if' introduces the condition; در 'the door/gate' is the direct object; ببندد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form 'closes' (classical/poetic subjunctive), and به ده سال is a duration phrase 'for ten years'; شاه is the agent 'the king' (postposed for emphasis).
segments.s397-g126: خورش هست چندانک باید سپاه is the apodosis: خورش هست 'there is food', چندانک 'to such an extent', and باید سپاه is an elliptic, poetic construction where باید (it is necessary / there will be need) is followed by سپاه (an army) = 'an army will be needed' or 'it will require an army.' Word order places the modal verb باید before the noun-complement without an explicit verb of existence.
segments.s397-g127: اگر خواهد از چین و ماچین سوار is a conditional/headline with synthetic future: اگر 'if', خواهد (3rd-person singular) expresses desire/will ('he wishes/will'), and از چین و ماچین specifies origin 'from China and Ma-chin' (a paired place-name expression). سوار 'horsemen' is the noun to be obtained. In classical Persian two verbs often frame the possibility (خواهد ... بیابد).
segments.s397-g128: بیابد برش نامور صد هزار is the result clause: بیابد (subjunctive/optative 3rd pers.) 'he will find/can find', برش here functions as 'for him' (poetic contraction) — the listed senses do not include this pronominal use so it is annotated as unmatched — and نامور modifies the numeral phrase 'صد هزار' = 'a hundred thousand famed (horsemen)'. The verb + indirect object + adjective-numeral sequence is typical poetic ordering.
segments.s397-g9: همی is a frequent poetic particle here (emphatic/progressive) marking immediate or ongoing action. پیش رو is a compound-like adverbial phrase meaning “forward / ahead” (پیش + رو: direction toward the front). غرقه گشت is a verbal phrase in past tense: غرقه (immersed) + گشت (became) = “became submerged.” اندر آب places the action inside the water (اندر = in, poetic).
segments.s397-g10: سپهبد is the subject (the commander); بزد is past simple of زدن used here as ‘struck’ or ‘smote.’ چنگ here means a striking/grasping with the hand/claw (poetic physical blow). هم = “also / too,” indicating an additional action; در شتاب is a locative/temporal phrase ‘in haste’ (در + شتاب). Word order puts the actor before the verb as normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s397-g11: گرفتش is گرفت + ـش (he seized him). دو ران = ‘both thighs’ (دو = two/both, ران = thigh). بر کشیدش combines the preposition-like بر with کشیدش (کشید + ـش = he pulled him), giving ‘pulled him up/on’ or ‘dragged him forth.’ ز گل is a poetic ablative ‘from the mud’ (ز = from, گل = mud/clay here).
segments.s397-g12: بترسید is past tense (he/it feared; third-person singular of ترسیدن). The following three words form a noun phrase describing who feared: بدخواه (ill-wisher / malevolent), ترک (a Turk) andچگل appears as an epithet or name (here lexicalized as a label; the line likely names the Turk). The sequence is nominative/comment: ‘the ill-wishing Turk Chagol was afraid.’
segments.s397-g13: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form (he commanded/said). تا here introduces a purpose/result clause (‘so that / until / in order that’). گرگسار is a compound adjective/noun meaning ‘wolf-infested / wolf-haunted (place)’. نژند is an adjective meaning ‘base / humbled / dishonored’ and completes the clause: ‘that the wolf-haunted place become humbled.’
segments.s397-g14: شود is the subjunctive/past-future auxiliary ‘become / let it be’ (here part of the commanded/result clause). داغ دل is a genitive phrase: داغ = burning grief/wound, دل = heart → ‘the heart’s burning sorrow.’ پیش بر پای بند is an expression where پیش = before/in front, بر = on/upon, پای = foot, بند = bond/fetter; together it describes binding that sorrow ‘before the foot’ (poetic positioning). The clause therefore issues or predicts a painful fate bound upon someone.
segments.s397-g15: بدو گفت is a common frame: بدو = ‘to him’ (indirect object), گفت = ‘he said’ (past of گفتن). کای is a vocative particle ‘O’ (addressing someone). ریمن is a proper name (Riman). گرگسار follows as a descriptive epithet (‘wolf-haunted’), here applied to the subject being referred to in the reported speech.
segments.s397-g16: گرفتار is a predicative adjective/participle meaning ‘captured / trapped.’ بر دست is an idiom meaning ‘in the hand/power of’ (بر = on/upon, دست = hand/power). اسفندیار is the agent/possessor name (Esfandiyar). Together: ‘(is) captured in Esfandiyar’s hand’ (i.e. held by Esfandiyar).
segments.s397-g33: نیازارم is a first-person verbal phrase; here it functions as the main clause verb (literally 'I will not harm/spare' in this archaic phrasing). آن را marks the definite object ('that one') with را as the object marker; که begins a relative clause 'which/that', and فرزند is the head noun 'child/son'. تست here is an archaic 2nd-person possessive/enclitic ('your'), placed after the noun (classical word-order contrasts with modern فرزندِ تو).
segments.s397-g34: هم is an adverb 'also/likewise' linking this clause to the previous object. آن را که introduces another definite relative object ('that which'), از is the preposition 'from', دوده 'soot', and پیوند here denotes a graft/attachment (literal or metaphorical 'grafted from soot'). تست again is the archaic 2nd-person possessive enclitic 'your'. The whole phrase is another object parallel to the first.
segments.s397-g35: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard' (3rd singular). گفتار او 'his speech/words' is the object of بشنید. The proper name گرگسار follows for poetic inversion (subject position indicated by name rather than a strict SVO order).
segments.s397-g36: پرامید شد is an adjectival phrase + copula 'became hopeful' (پرامید = full of hope, شد = became). جانش is 'his life' (جان + 3rd-person enclitic -ش) serving as the theme affected, and از before شهریار indicates cause/agent here ('because of / by the king'). The clause states the result (he became hopeful) and gives its cause.
segments.s397-g37: ز (poetic form of از) means 'from' or 'because of' and introduces the source: گفتار او 'his speech/words'. ماند is the past of ماندن 'remained/was left', and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/amazement' (اندر = 'in', شگفت = 'wonder'). Word order places the source and manner before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s397-g38: زمین را uses را as the definite object marker ('the ground'). ببوسید is simple past 'he kissed'. و links the second coordinated verb phrase پوزش گرفت: پوزش 'pardon/apology' and گرفت 'took/received' — an idiom meaning 'he sought/asked for pardon' (literally 'he took pardon').
segments.s397-g39: بدو ('to him') marks the indirect object; گفت شاه literally 'said the king' (inverted order common in classical poetry). آنچ گفتی is a fused relative phrase 'that which you said / what you said' (آنچ = 'that which', گفتی = 'you said' or nominalized speech), and گذشت is a perfect/past notion 'has passed / is past/forgiven.' The segment reports the king's address.
segments.s397-g40: ز گفتار خامت begins with ز ('from') indicating source; گفتار 'speech' + خامت (خام + -ت, 'raw/immature' + 2nd-person enclitic) = 'your raw/naive words'. نگشت is the negative/contrary past 'did not become/turn', and آب دشت 'the water of the plain' is the affected locus; idiomatically the line says the addressee's naive speech had no effect on the plain's waters (i.e., it changed nothing).
segments.s397-g129: نیازش (literally “his need”) is used impersonally to mean “he has no need.” نیابد is a negative present/optative form of یافتن (here effectively “does not have/need”); together نیازش نیابد = “he has no need.” The two prepositional phrases به چیزی به کس follow the verb and specify scope/source: به چیزی = “of anything” and به کس = “from anyone” (poetic word order places these after the verb).
segments.s397-g130: خورش هست is a nominal clause with the copula هست (“there is/is present”), here meaning “he has food” (خورش = cooked food/stew). The conjunction و links a second nominal phrase مردان فریادرس, where مردان is the plural noun “men” (warriors/companions) and فریادرس is an attributive noun/adjective “rescuers” or “those who answer a cry”; the verb is omitted in the second member (elliptical) so the sense is “and men to come to his aid.”
segments.s397-g131: چو ... گفتند is a temporal clause meaning “when they said/it was said”; چو = “when.” گفتند is the past plural (“they said”); the reported content follows: او تیغ هندی به مشت = “he (او) [had] an Indian (هندی) sword (تیغ) in his fist” where به مشت indicates possession/position “in/at the fist/hand.” Word order places the reported clause after the reporting verb, a common epic style.
segments.s397-g132: دو گردنکش ساده‌دل را is the definite direct object (را marks the object): “two گردنکش (headstrong/rebellious) simple‑hearted men.” The verb بکشت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning “he killed” (the b‑ prefix is an older narrative verbal form attached to the root کشت “kill”); together the clause means he killed those two men.
segments.s397-g57: سر جنگجویان is a noun phrase with سر ('chief, head') governing جنگجویان ('of the warriors'); the clause ends with نشست, past tense 'sat'. The construction به + infinitive (به خوردن) marks the action they begin/undertake ('to eat'), so the literal order is 'heads of the warriors to eating sat' = 'the leaders sat down to eat.'
segments.s397-g58: شد here is the past of شدن used as a copula/phase verb 'became / was' and creates a verbless predicate with پرستنده ('a devotee / worshipper') as the predicate noun; جام باده به دست is an adverbial phrase 'with a cup of wine in (his) hand' modifying that state. The subject is omitted but recovered from context (a man present at the table).
segments.s397-g59: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. The conjunction تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that' / 'so that' (he ordered that ...). The list جوشن و خود و گبر are coordinated direct objects ('the cuirass, himself, and the Gabr').
segments.s397-g60: ببردند is used inside the تا-clause as an optative/subjunctive-like past 'that they take/bring' (here 'should take/bring'); با تیغ is an instrumental phrase 'with sword(s)'; پیش هژبر means 'before/in the presence of Hežbar' (prepositional phrase indicating destination/appearance before that person).
segments.s397-g61: گشاده here modifies بفرمود adverbially ('openly / publicly declared'), and تا again introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. گرگسار is used as a noun/epithet 'the ferocious one' (a fierce man) who is being summoned.
segments.s397-g62: بیامد is past 'came'; به پیش is a prepositional expression 'to the presence of / before'; یل اسفندیار is the addressee/goal 'the hero Esfandiyar.' The verb plus به پیش + personal name means 'came before [so-and-so].'
segments.s397-g63: بدو گفت is the common sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him' + گفت = 'said'). کاکنون is an emphatic/archaic temporal adverb 'now / at this moment.' گذشتی is 2nd-person past 'you have passed' (literally 'you passed'), followed by ز بد 'from/beyond evil' — i.e. 'you have moved beyond evil.'
segments.s397-g64: ز تو here marks source/reference 'with you / as for you'; خوبی و راست گفتن is a coordinated nominal phrase 'goodness and telling the truth'; سزد is an impersonal/archaic present 'it befits / it is fitting.' The clause therefore states that goodness and honesty befit you.
segments.s397-g105: دریغ is an exclamatory interjection ('alas'/'what a pity') that opens the line. The noun phrase این همه رنج و پیکار ما follows it: این همه = 'all these' (quantifier + demonstrative), رنج و پیکار = a coordinated noun phrase 'suffering and battle', and ما is the free pronoun functioning as a possessive 'our' (poetic placement after the nouns rather than with an ezāfe).
segments.s397-g106: پشیمانی آمد is a verbal phrase 'regret came' (آمد here a simple past 'came/happened'). همه کار ما is a noun phrase 'all our affairs/deeds' and is the affected domain: syntactically the verb phrase precedes the affected NP for poetic word order, so the line reads 'regret came upon all our affairs.'
segments.s397-g107: به گرد بیابان is a prepositional phrase 'around the plain/desert' (به + گرد = 'around'). همه modifies the understood subject ('they all') and بنگرید is an archaic/polite past form (classical third-person plural/past) meaning 'they looked' or 'they gazed'; poetic word order places the verb after its adverbial phrase.
segments.s397-g108: دو ترک is the direct object 'two Turks.' اندران دشت is a locative phrase 'in that plain/therein the plain' (اندران = 'in/among that'), and پوینده is a present participle 'moving' that modifies the Turks. دید is the past verb 'saw' (again in a compressed poetic past form). Together: 'they saw two Turks moving in that plain.'
segments.s397-g109: همی رفت is a classical progressive past construction: همی (verbal prefix indicating continuous/habitual action) + رفت 'went' = 'was going' / 'ran on.' پیش اندرون is a directional phrase 'ahead / toward the inner (part),' and چار سگ is the direct object 'four dogs' (چار = 'four').
segments.s397-g110: سگانی که ... is a relative clause modifying the dogs: سگانی 'the hounds' + که 'that/who' + گیرند (classical present/plural form) 'they seize/catch' + آهو 'the gazelle.' به تگ is an adverbial/instrumental phrase attached to گیرند; poetically it indicates the manner/place (here rendered 'in a narrow spot'). The verb is general/habitual rather than a single perfect event.
segments.s397-g111: ز بالا فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression: ز بالا 'from above' + فرود آمد 'descended' (فرود as noun/verb 'descent' plus آمد 'came' forming a past action). اسفندیار follows the verb for emphatic/poetic ordering as the subject 'Esfandiyar (he) descended.'
segments.s397-g112: به چنگ اندرون نیزه کارزار is a prepositional noun phrase describing how he descended: به + چنگ 'into/with (in) the grasp' (چنگ here 'claw/hold; figuratively 'grip' or 'hand'), اندرون modifies the relation 'within his grip,' and نیزه کارزار is a compound 'spear of battle' (نیزه modified by کارزار). Together: 'with the battle-spear in his grasp.'

sentences.s397-s49: Three parasangs high and forty in breadth — within it he saw no water or mud anywhere.
sentences.s397-s50: Across the width of its wall four horsemen rode abreast.
sentences.s397-s51: When Esfandiyar saw that marvel, he drew up a cold wind from his heart.
sentences.s397-s52: He said thus: 'This must not be seized; misfortune has befallen me by an evil way.'
sentences.s397-s37: I will make all their graves a feast for the lions; I will make them the satisfaction of Iran’s brave men.
